RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues shirt is a defective product, cites case law, requests leave to amend. Defendant responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)
The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT BROOKS BROTHERS GROUP, INC.'s Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling with a modification.

Defendant Brooks Brothers' motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with 20 days' leave to amend as to the first cause of action for negligence, the second and third causes of action for strict liability and breach of implied warranty and without leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action for breach of express warranty.

Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with leave to amend as to the cause of action for negligence, and as to the cause of action for strict liability. Plaintiff has not adequately pled a legal duty under the Rowland factors that could serve as a basis for its negligence or strict products liability/duty to warn claims. (Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108.) Plaintiff ignores Defendant's analysis of this key threshold issue. The Court finds that defendant did not have a duty to warn of the risk that a malfunctioning hairdryer emitting "sparks and flame" might ignite a 100% cotton shirt. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Proposed Order; And Declaration Of James F. Peterson Regarding Compliance With Meet And Confer Requirements Of Code Of Civil Procedure 435.5 (A)
Off Calendar Nov-13-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BROOKS BROTHERS GROUP, INC.'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint;

Defendant Brooks Brothers Group, Inc.'s motion to strike portions of the complaint is granted as to all references to death and wrongful death and denied as to the allegations and prayer for punitive damages. Plaintiff Hiram Borunda acknowledges that the references to death and wrongful death should be stricken. Liberally construed, the complaint alleges that Brooks Brothers: 1) knowingly sold a dangerous and defective shirt due to its lack of needed fire retardant and fire resistant features; 2) knew that the shirt could easily catch and spread a fire causing serious injuries to any person who wore the shirt or had it in his possession; and 3) knowingly failed to warn of these issues and to take any other measures to protect the safety of persons who wore the shirt or had in their possessions. These allegations are sufficient to support punitive damages liability based on despicable conduct carried on with a willful and conscious disregard for the safety of others.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
